expanded_description:
The money supply is the total stock of money circulating in an economy at a given point in time. But what counts as 'money'? Different assets serve monetary functions to varying degrees: currency and checkable deposits are perfectly liquid and serve as a medium of exchange; savings deposits and money market funds are less liquid but can be quickly converted to spending. Central banks track multiple measures of the money supply—narrow money (M1) and broad money (M2)—to gauge liquidity conditions, predict inflation, and guide monetary policy.

Understanding money supply measures is essential for interpreting central bank reports, analyzing the relationship between money growth and inflation, and evaluating the transmission of monetary policy. M1 includes the most liquid assets (currency in circulation, demand deposits, traveler's checks, and other checkable deposits). M2 adds near-monies: savings deposits, small time deposits, and retail money market mutual funds. The distinction between M1 and M2 matters because different components have different velocities and different responses to interest rates.

In this section, we will define M1 and M2, examine the components of each aggregate, analyze how financial innovation has blurred the boundaries between narrow and broad money, compare money supply measures across countries, and trace how central banks use these measures in policy decisions. You will learn to calculate M1 and M2 from balance sheet data, interpret trends in the components, and understand why the relationship between money supply and inflation has weakened since the 1980s due to financial innovation and deregulation.

core_concept.principle:
The money supply is measured using hierarchical aggregates based on liquidity. The two primary measures are:

M1 (Narrow Money): The most liquid forms of money, directly usable as a medium of exchange.
• Currency in circulation (coins and paper money held by the public, excluding bank vault cash)
• Demand deposits (checkable deposits at commercial banks, no interest or low interest)
• Traveler's checks (issued by non-banks, now small but historically significant)
• Other checkable deposits (NOW accounts, ATS accounts, credit union share draft accounts)

M2 (Broad Money): M1 plus near-monies that are less liquid but can be quickly converted to cash or checking deposits.
• M1 (all components above)
• Savings deposits (including money market deposit accounts, or MMDAs)
• Small time deposits (certificates of deposit under \$100,000)
• Retail money market mutual funds (MMMFs, held by individuals, not institutions)

Key Formulas:
• M1 = Currency + Demand Deposits + Traveler's Checks + Other Checkable Deposits
• M2 = M1 + Savings Deposits + Small Time Deposits + Retail MMMFs

Excluded from M2: Large time deposits (\$100,000+), institutional money market funds, repurchase agreements, Eurodollars. These are tracked separately (M3, discontinued by the Fed in 2006).

Liquidity ordering: Currency (most liquid) > Demand deposits > Savings deposits > Small time deposits (least liquid among M2).
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine you have three assets: a \$20 bill in your wallet, a checking account with \$1,000, and a savings account with \$5,000. The \$20 bill and the \$1,000 in checking are M1—you can spend them instantly without any conversion. The \$5,000 in savings is not M1 (you cannot write a check directly from most savings accounts), but it is M2 because you can transfer it to checking instantly via mobile banking or ATM.

Now suppose you have a \$10,000 certificate of deposit (CD) that matures in 6 months. This is included in M2 but not M1. You cannot spend it until maturity (or you pay an early withdrawal penalty). A large corporation holds a \$500,000 institutional money market fund—this is excluded even from M2 (formerly part of M3).

The distinction between M1 and M2 has blurred over time. In the 1980s, NOW accounts (interest-bearing checking) blurred the line between demand deposits (M1) and savings (M2). In 2020, the Fed eliminated reserve requirements, making the distinction less relevant for policy. Yet M1 and M2 remain useful for understanding liquidity conditions and forecasting inflation over long horizons.

image.alt: Pyramid diagram: Base is Currency (most liquid), then Demand Deposits, then Savings Deposits, then Small Time Deposits (least liquid); M1 outlined at top two layers; M2 outlined including all layers.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.1: Hierarchical structure of money supply measures: M1 includes the most liquid assets; M2 adds near-monies with slightly lower liquidity.

sections.section_title: 7.1 Money Supply Measures: M1 and M2
sections.introduction:
The money supply is the total stock of money circulating in an economy at a given point in time. But what counts as 'money'? Different assets serve monetary functions to varying degrees: currency and checkable deposits are perfectly liquid and serve as a medium of exchange; savings deposits and money market funds are less liquid but can be quickly converted to spending. Central banks track multiple measures of the money supply—narrow money (M1) and broad money (M2)—to gauge liquidity conditions, predict inflation, and guide monetary policy.

Understanding money supply measures is essential for interpreting central bank reports, analyzing the relationship between money growth and inflation, and evaluating the transmission of monetary policy. M1 includes the most liquid assets (currency in circulation, demand deposits, traveler's checks, and other checkable deposits). M2 adds near-monies: savings deposits, small time deposits, and retail money market mutual funds. The distinction between M1 and M2 matters because different components have different velocities and different responses to interest rates.

In this section, we will define M1 and M2, examine the components of each aggregate, analyze how financial innovation has blurred the boundaries between narrow and broad money, compare money supply measures across countries, and trace how central banks use these measures in policy decisions. You will learn to calculate M1 and M2 from balance sheet data, interpret trends in the components, and understand why the relationship between money supply and inflation has weakened since the 1980s due to financial innovation and deregulation.

introduction_image.alt: Pie charts: M1 broken down into Currency (40%), Demand Deposits (50%), Other Checkable Deposits (10%); M2 broken down into M1 (25%), Savings Deposits (55%), Small Time Deposits (12%), Retail MMMFs (8%).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.1.2: Composition of M1 and M2 (typical pre-2020 proportions); savings deposits dominate M2, while currency and demand deposits dominate M1.

key_concept.definition:
Money supply measures are aggregates that quantify the total stock of monetary assets in an economy, categorized by liquidity. The two primary measures are:

M1 (Narrow Money): The most liquid forms of money, directly usable as a medium of exchange.
• Currency in circulation: Coins and paper money held by the public (excludes vault cash held by banks).
• Demand deposits: Checkable deposits at commercial banks (non-interest-bearing or low-interest).
• Traveler's checks: Non-bank-issued checks (American Express, Visa); now a very small component.
• Other checkable deposits: NOW accounts, ATS accounts, credit union share draft accounts (interest-bearing checking).

M2 (Broad Money): M1 plus near-monies that are less liquid but can be quickly converted to cash or checking deposits.
• Savings deposits: Including money market deposit accounts (MMDAs) offered by banks.
• Small time deposits: Certificates of deposit (CDs) under \$100,000.
• Retail money market mutual funds (MMMFs): Held by individuals (not institutional).

Excluded from M2: Large time deposits (\$100,000+), institutional MMMFs, repurchase agreements, Eurodollars. The Fed discontinued M3 in 2006, citing that it added little predictive power beyond M2.

Key Formulas:
• M1 = Currency + Demand Deposits + Traveler's Checks + Other Checkable Deposits
• M2 = M1 + Savings Deposits + Small Time Deposits + Retail MMMFs
key_concept.context: The distinction between M1 and M2 emerged in the 1970s as financial innovation created new assets that functioned like money but were not checkable. The Fed began publishing M2 in 1971. The 1980s saw the introduction of NOW accounts (interest-bearing checking) and money market deposit accounts, blurring the M1-M2 boundary. In 2020, the Fed eliminated reserve requirements, reducing the policy relevance of M1. However, M2 remains a key indicator of broad liquidity. The relationship between M2 growth and inflation has weakened since the 1990s due to financial innovation and globalization. Other countries use different measures: the ECB uses M1, M2, and M3 (including repurchase agreements); the Bank of England uses M0 (narrow) and M4 (broad).

image.alt: Horizontal spectrum from 'Most Liquid' to 'Least Liquid': Currency → Demand Deposits → Savings Deposits → Small Time Deposits → Large Time Deposits (excluded from M2); M1 bracket covers first two; M2 bracket covers first four.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.3: Liquidity spectrum of monetary assets; M1 includes the most liquid, M2 extends to near-monies, and assets beyond M2 are excluded from standard aggregates.

classifications.value: Narrow money: currency and checkable deposits directly usable for transactions
characteristics: Currency in circulation: Approximately \$2.2 trillion in the U.S. (2024)
Demand deposits: Non-interest-bearing checking accounts at commercial banks
Other checkable deposits: NOW accounts, ATS, credit union share drafts
Traveler's checks: Declining from over \$8 billion in 1990 to under \$1 billion today
M1 velocity (GDP/M1) has fallen sharply since the 1990s
classifications.behavior:
M1 is the traditional measure of money used for transactions. Its components have changed dramatically over time. In the 1960s, currency and demand deposits dominated M1. The 1970s introduced NOW accounts (interest-bearing checking), shifting funds from savings (M2) into M1. The 1990s saw the rise of money market funds, which pulled funds out of M1 into M2.

In 2020, M1 exploded as the Fed injected reserves and banks created deposits, while the public increased checking balances due to pandemic uncertainty. M1 grew from \$4 trillion in early 2020 to over \$18 trillion by 2022—a 350% increase. This was partly due to a redefinition by the Fed: in May 2020, the Fed eliminated the distinction between demand deposits and other checkable deposits, and moved savings deposits into M1 (blurring the M1-M2 distinction further).

The velocity of M1 (how many times a dollar is spent per year) fell from over 7 in 1990 to under 1.5 in 2023. This means that despite massive M1 growth, nominal GDP did not increase proportionally—the extra money was held as idle balances, not spent. This weakened the link between money supply and inflation.

image.alt: Line graph (1990-2024): M1 total rising sharply after 2020; currency steady; demand deposits spiking; savings deposits moving into M1 after 2020 redefinition.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.4: U.S. M1 components over time; the 2020 redefinition moved savings deposits into M1, causing a structural break in the series.

examples.process: Calculating M1 from Balance Sheet Data
examples.explanation: A country reports: Currency = \$1,200 billion, Demand deposits = \$800 billion, Traveler's checks = \$5 billion, Other checkable deposits = \$400 billion. M1 = 1,200 + 800 + 5 + 400 = \$2,405 billion.

examples.process: Impact of NOW Accounts on M1
examples.explanation: In 1980, a household moves \$10,000 from a non-interest-bearing checking account (already M1) to a NOW account (interest-bearing checking). M1 is unchanged—both are checkable. But if the household moves \$10,000 from a savings account (M2 only) to a NOW account, M1 increases by \$10,000 and M2 is unchanged (savings decreases, M1 increases by same amount).

classifications.value: Broad money: M1 plus savings deposits, small time deposits, and retail money market funds
characteristics: Savings deposits: Largest component of M2 (over 50% pre-2020, now redefined)
Small time deposits: CDs under \$100,000, declining share due to low rates
Retail MMMFs: Held by individuals, sensitive to interest rate differentials
M2 grew steadily from \$4 trillion (1990) to \$21 trillion (2024)
M2 velocity has been relatively stable compared to M1
classifications.behavior:
M2 is the broad measure most closely watched by economists and central banks. It correlates more strongly with nominal GDP and inflation over long horizons than M1. The components of M2 have shifted over time: savings deposits dominated in the 1980s-1990s; money market funds grew rapidly in the 2000s; time deposits shrank as interest rates fell after 2008.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, M2 grew at historically high rates (over 25% year-over-year in 2020-2021). This was driven by: (1) massive fiscal transfers (stimulus checks) deposited into bank accounts; (2) Fed QE creating reserves that became deposits; (3) reduced consumption (lockdowns) leading to higher savings. Many economists warned that high M2 growth would lead to inflation. Indeed, inflation surged to 9% in 2022—a lag of about 18 months, consistent with historical patterns.

However, the M2-inflation relationship is not stable. In the 1990s, M2 grew rapidly but inflation remained low (the 'missing money' puzzle). Explanations include: financial innovation (new near-monies not in M2), globalization (imported disinflation), and changes in velocity. The Fed largely stopped targeting M2 after 1990, shifting to interest rate rules (Taylor rule).

image.alt: Stacked area graph (1990-2024): M2 total rising; savings deposits largest; small time deposits shrinking after 2008; retail MMMFs growing then shrinking.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.5: U.S. M2 components over time; savings deposits dominate, but small time deposits have declined as interest rates fell.

examples.process: Calculating M2 from M1 and Additions
examples.explanation: Given M1 = \$2,405 billion, Savings deposits = \$10,000 billion, Small time deposits = \$1,200 billion, Retail MMMFs = \$800 billion. M2 = 2,405 + 10,000 + 1,200 + 800 = \$14,405 billion.

When the Fed raises interest rates, money market fund yields rise faster than bank savings deposit rates. Households may shift funds from savings deposits (M2) to MMMFs (also M2—no change in M2). But if they shift to institutional MMMFs (excluded from M2), M2 falls, representing a leakage from broad money.

classifications.value: Different central banks define money supply aggregates differently based on their financial systems
characteristics: Federal Reserve: M1, M2 (discontinued M3 in 2006)
European Central Bank: M1 (narrow), M2 (intermediate), M3 (broad including repos)
Bank of England: M0 (narrow, notes/coins + reserves), M4 (broad)
Bank of Japan: M1, M2, M3, broad liquidity
People's Bank of China: M0 (currency), M1 (currency + demand deposits), M2 (M1 + savings + time deposits)
classifications.behavior:
Comparing money supply measures across countries is difficult because definitions differ. The ECB's M3 includes repurchase agreements and money market fund shares/units, which the Fed excludes. The Bank of England's M4 includes building society deposits and commercial paper. China's M2 includes all deposits (household and corporate), making it much larger relative to GDP than in Western economies.

Despite definitional differences, cross-country patterns emerge: M2-to-GDP ratios are higher in countries with bank-based financial systems (Germany, China, Japan) and lower in market-based systems (US, UK). The velocity of money tends to decline with financial development and aging populations.

International organizations (IMF, BIS) use standardized measures for cross-country comparisons, but analysts must be cautious. The growth rate of M2, not the level, is often used to gauge monetary policy stance—rapid M2 growth may signal loose policy, but also could reflect financial deepening or dollarization (foreign currency deposits included in M2).

image.alt: Bar chart: M2 as % of GDP for selected countries (2023): China (200%), Japan (180%), Eurozone (120%), US (90%), UK (80%); China highest due to bank-based system and high savings rate.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.6: M2-to-GDP ratios vary widely across countries, reflecting differences in financial structure, payment systems, and savings behavior.

examples.explanation: ECB M3 = M2 + repurchase agreements + money market fund shares/units + debt securities up to 2 years. This broader measure captures more near-monies than the Fed's M2, making Eurozone M3-to-GDP ratios appear higher than US M2-to-GDP even when underlying liquidity is similar.

China's M2 includes all deposits. However, a large shadow banking system (wealth management products, trust loans) operates outside M2. When households shift from bank deposits to wealth management products, M2 falls even though total liquidity hasn't changed, misleading policymakers. The PBOC now monitors 'total social financing' as a broader measure.

principles.principle: Money Supply Aggregates Are Based on Liquidity, Not Legal Tender Status
principles.statement: An asset is included in M1 if it can be used directly as a medium of exchange with minimal transaction costs and no time delay. Currency has legal tender status, but checkable deposits are not legal tender—they are promises by banks to pay legal tender on demand. Yet they function as money because they are widely accepted. Conversely, some legal tender (e.g., large denomination notes hoarded as stores of value) may not circulate as money. The liquidity-based definition means that financial innovation can create new forms of money (e.g., crypto stablecoins, mobile money) that may or may not be included in official aggregates depending on their acceptance and convertibility.
principles.application: When evaluating whether a new financial instrument (e.g., a stablecoin, a mobile wallet balance) should be considered 'money,' ask: (1) Can it be used directly to make payments to a third party? (2) Is it convertible at par into currency on demand? (3) Is there minimal transaction cost and delay? If yes, it functions as money even if not legal tender. Central banks may need to revise their money supply definitions as payment systems evolve. The rise of FedNow (instant payments) and stablecoins may blur definitions further.
principles.example: PayPal and Venmo balances are not included in M1 or M2 because they are not bank deposits. Yet millions of consumers use them to make payments instantly. The BIS has proposed a new measure, 'digital M1,' to include such balances. Similarly, El Salvador's adoption of Bitcoin as legal tender raises questions: should Bitcoin in circulation be counted as part of M1? Currently, no central bank includes crypto in official money supply measures.

image.alt: Continuum from 'Pure Money' (currency, demand deposits) to 'Near Money' (savings, time deposits) to 'Non-Money' (stocks, real estate); stablecoins and mobile money placed near the pure money end.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.7: The liquidity continuum: money is defined by function, not form. Financial innovation continuously creates new assets that blur traditional boundaries.

principles.principle: The Relationship Between Money Supply and Inflation Is Unstable Over Time
principles.statement: The quantity theory of money ($MV = PY$) predicts a proportional relationship between money supply growth and inflation. Empirically, this relationship held in the 1970s and 1980s but broke down in the 1990s and 2000s. The velocity of money (V) is not constant; it varies with interest rates, financial innovation, and uncertainty. M2 velocity fell from 2.2 in 1990 to 1.2 in 2023, meaning each dollar of M2 supports less nominal spending. Central banks have largely abandoned money supply targeting (the 'monetarist experiment' of 1979-1982 was abandoned after unstable velocity made it unreliable). However, extreme money growth (e.g., 2020-2021 M2 growth of 25%+) still predicts inflation with long lags, as seen in 2022.
principles.application: Do not rely on simple correlations between M2 growth and inflation for short-term forecasting. Velocity shocks (e.g., pandemic hoarding of money) can decouple money from spending. Instead, monitor: (1) the components of M2 (are households holding idle balances or spending them?); (2) credit growth (banks creating deposits via lending); (3) velocity measures. For policy, the Fed now uses interest rates (Taylor rule) rather than money supply targets. But rapid M2 growth remains a warning sign of potential future inflation if velocity normalizes.
principles.example: From 1990 to 2000, M2 grew at 5-6% annually but inflation averaged 2-3%. Velocity fell, absorbing the extra money. From 2020-2021, M2 grew at 25% annually; velocity fell further but could not absorb all the growth. By 2022, velocity stabilized and inflation surged to 9%. The lag was about 18 months—consistent with historical patterns but unpredictable in timing and magnitude.

image.alt: Scatter plot (1960-2024): M2 growth (x-axis) vs. inflation (y-axis); strong positive correlation in 1970s; weak correlation in 1990s-2010s; points for 2020-2022 showing high M2 growth followed by high inflation.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.8: The M2-inflation relationship has weakened since the 1990s but reappeared after the 2020-2021 money surge, suggesting the relationship is nonlinear and time-varying.

worked_examples.title: Calculating M1 and M2 from Economic Data

image.alt: Table: Currency = \$1,500B, Demand deposits = \$900B, Traveler's checks = \$4B, Other checkable = \$350B, Savings deposits = \$8,000B, Small time deposits = \$1,000B, Retail MMMFs = \$600B. M1 and M2 calculated below.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.9: Worked example: computing M1 and M2 from component data.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy reports the following monetary aggregates (in billions of \$):

• Currency held by public: \$1,500
• Demand deposits at commercial banks: \$900
• Traveler's checks outstanding: \$4
• Other checkable deposits (NOW, ATS): \$350
• Savings deposits (including MMDAs): \$8,000
• Small time deposits (CDs under \$100,000): \$1,000
• Retail money market mutual funds: \$600
• Institutional money market funds: \$1,200
• Large time deposits (over \$100,000): \$2,000
• Repurchase agreements: \$500

a) Calculate M1.
b) Calculate M2.
c) Explain why institutional money market funds are excluded from M2.
d) If the central bank redefines M2 to include retail MMMFs only (already done), how does that affect the calculation?
e) Suppose households shift \$200 billion from savings deposits to checking accounts. How do M1 and M2 change?

step_by_step.explanation:
M1 = Currency + Demand deposits + Traveler's checks + Other checkable deposits
M1 = 1,500 + 900 + 4 + 350 = \$2,754 billion

M2 = M1 + Savings deposits + Small time deposits + Retail MMMFs
M2 = 2,754 + 8,000 + 1,000 + 600 = \$12,354 billion

Note: Institutional MMMFs (\$1,200B), large time deposits (\$2,000B), and repos (\$500B) are excluded from M2.

Institutional MMMFs are excluded from M2 because they are held by large institutions (corporations, governments, pension funds), not retail households. Institutional investors use MMMFs as cash management tools, not as transaction balances. They are less stable (more prone to runs, as in 2008 when the Reserve Primary Fund 'broke the buck') and have higher minimum investments (typically \$1 million+). The Fed tracks them separately in M3 (discontinued) or as part of broader liquidity measures. Their exclusion from M2 is based on the assumption that institutional holdings do not directly affect household spending and inflation.

Retail MMMFs (\$600B in this example) are included in M2 because they are held by individuals, have check-writing privileges (often with limits, e.g., \$500 minimum check), and are used as transaction balances. In 2006, the Fed reclassified some MMMFs from M2 to M3 and back based on redemption features. Currently, all retail MMMFs are in M2. The distinction is based on the fund's investor base, not the asset type.

step_by_step.step: Analyze shift from savings to checking
step_by_step.explanation:
Households shift \$200 billion from savings deposits to checking accounts.

Before shift: Savings deposits = \$8,000B, Checking (part of M1) = demand deposits (\$900B) + other checkable (\$350B) = \$1,250B.

After shift: Savings deposits = \$7,800B (down \$200B); Checking = \$1,450B (up \$200B).

Change in M1: M1 includes checking but not savings. So M1 increases by \$200B (from \$2,754B to \$2,954B).

Change in M2: M2 includes both checking and savings. The increase in checking (+\$200B) exactly offsets the decrease in savings (-\$200B). So M2 is unchanged (still \$12,354B).

This illustrates a key point: shifts between M2 components do not change M2; only injections of new reserves (open market operations) or changes in bank lending can change M2.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) M1 = \$2,754 billion. b) M2 = \$12,354 billion. c) Institutional MMMFs are excluded because they are held by large institutions, have high minimum investments, and are less stable. d) Already included (retail only). e) M1 increases by \$200B; M2 unchanged.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying definitions of M1 and M2 components, distinguishing retail vs. institutional funds, and analyzing how shifts between components affect aggregates.

worked_examples.title: Velocity of Money and the Quantity Equation

image.alt: Table: Year 1: M2 = \$10T, GDP = \$20T, Velocity = 2.0; Year 2: M2 = \$12T, GDP = \$21T, Velocity = 1.75; calculations shown.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.10: Velocity of money calculation using the quantity equation MV = PY.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has the following data:

• Year 1: M2 = \$10 trillion, Nominal GDP = \$20 trillion
• Year 2: M2 = \$12 trillion, Nominal GDP = \$21 trillion
• Year 3: M2 = \$13 trillion, Nominal GDP = \$22 trillion

a) Calculate the velocity of M2 for each year (V = PY / M).
b) Interpret the trend in velocity.
c) If velocity had remained constant at Year 1 levels, what would nominal GDP have been in Year 2 given the actual M2?
d) What factors might explain a declining velocity of money?
e) Using the quantity theory, if the central bank wants to achieve 3% inflation and potential output grows at 2%, what money growth rate should it target (assuming constant velocity)?

step_by_step.explanation:
Velocity (V) = Nominal GDP (PY) / Money Supply (M)

Year 1: V1 = \$20T / \$10T = 2.00
Year 2: V2 = \$21T / \$12T = 1.75
Year 3: V3 = \$22T / \$13T = 1.69

Velocity is falling from 2.00 to 1.75 to 1.69. This means each dollar of M2 supports less nominal spending over time. Velocity fell by 12.5% from Year 1 to Year 2, then by another 3.4% from Year 2 to Year 3. Declining velocity is common in developed economies due to: (1) financial innovation (more near-monies not in M2); (2) aging population (older households hold more money relative to spending); (3) increased uncertainty (precautionary money holdings); (4) falling interest rates (lower opportunity cost of holding money).

step_by_step.step: Calculate counterfactual GDP with constant velocity
step_by_step.explanation:
If velocity remained constant at Year 1 level (V = 2.00), then from MV = PY:

Year 2 counterfactual PY = M2 × V = \$12T × 2.00 = \$24 trillion

Actual Year 2 GDP was \$21 trillion. The difference (\$3 trillion) represents the 'missing spending' due to velocity decline. This illustrates why rapid money growth does not always cause proportional inflation—velocity can absorb some of the increase.

Factors that reduce money velocity:
• Financial innovation: New assets (money market funds, repos) serve as stores of value but are excluded from M2, so M2 velocity falls even if total liquidity velocity is stable.
• Falling interest rates: Lower opportunity cost of holding money encourages larger idle balances, reducing velocity.
• Uncertainty: Recessions and financial crises cause precautionary hoarding of money (increase in savings deposits), reducing velocity.
• Aging population: Older households have higher money balances relative to income, reducing velocity.
• Payment system improvements: Faster payments (FedNow) might increase velocity, but evidence is mixed.

Quantity theory (in growth rates): %ΔM + %ΔV = %ΔP + %ΔY

Assume constant velocity (%ΔV = 0). Target inflation (%ΔP) = 3%. Potential output growth (%ΔY) = 2%.

Then: %ΔM + 0 = 3% + 2% = 5%

The central bank should target 5% money supply growth to achieve 3% inflation with 2% real growth.

This is the basic monetarist policy rule (Friedman's k-percent rule). In practice, central banks do not target money supply because velocity is unstable. The Fed instead targets interest rates (federal funds rate) directly.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) V1 = 2.00, V2 = 1.75, V3 = 1.69. b) Velocity is declining. c) Counterfactual GDP = \$24 trillion. d) Financial innovation, falling interest rates, uncertainty, aging population. e) 5% money growth target (constant velocity assumption).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the quantity equation (MV = PY) to calculate velocity, interpret trends, and derive money growth targets under the quantity theory of money.

process_flow.process: Central bank collects data from financial institutions
process_flow.description: The Fed and other central banks gather balance sheet data from commercial banks, thrifts, credit unions, and money market funds to estimate money supply components.
process_flow.mechanism: Weekly reports (FR 2900 for the Fed) from depository institutions provide data on deposits, vault cash, and other liabilities. The Fed also surveys money market funds and uses Treasury data on currency in circulation.

image.alt: Data flow: Banks report deposits to Fed; Treasury reports currency; money market funds report assets; all aggregated to produce M1 and M2 statistics.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.11: Step 1 - Data collection: central banks aggregate balance sheet data from thousands of financial institutions to estimate money supply.

process_flow.process: Aggregation into M1 and M2 components
process_flow.description: Raw data are classified into components (currency, demand deposits, savings deposits, etc.) and summed to produce M1 and M2.
process_flow.mechanism: Statistical adjustments are made for seasonal factors, double-counting, and definitional boundaries (e.g., distinguishing retail vs. institutional MMMFs). The Fed publishes weekly H.6 release with seasonally adjusted and not-seasonally-adjusted aggregates.

image.alt: Aggregation flowchart: Currency + Demand deposits + Traveler's checks + Other checkable = M1; M1 + Savings + Small time + Retail MMMFs = M2.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.12: Step 2 - Aggregation: components are summed hierarchically to produce M1 and M2 aggregates.

process_flow.description: Money supply data are released weekly (Thursday at 4:30 PM for the Fed) and analyzed by economists, traders, and policymakers.
process_flow.mechanism: Markets react to unexpected changes in M1 and M2, though the impact is smaller than for employment or inflation data. Large deviations from trend may signal future inflation or changes in financial conditions.

image.alt: Release timeline: Data collected Monday-Tuesday; processed Wednesday; released Thursday 4:30 PM; analysts produce forecasts; markets adjust.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.13: Step 3 - Public release: money supply data are published on a schedule, allowing markets and policymakers to assess liquidity conditions.

process_flow.process: Central bank uses money aggregates in policy assessment
process_flow.description: While the Fed no longer targets money supply, it monitors M2 growth as one of many indicators of future inflation and financial stability.
process_flow.mechanism: The Fed's Monetary Policy Report discusses money supply trends. Rapid M2 growth relative to nominal GDP may signal excess liquidity and potential inflation. Slow M2 growth may signal deflationary pressures.

image.alt: Policy flow: M2 growth compared to nominal GDP growth → divergence signals potential inflation or deflation → Fed adjusts interest rates or QE.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.14: Step 4 - Policy assessment: central banks monitor money supply as one indicator among many, though interest rates remain the primary tool.

process_flow.process: Periodic redefinition of aggregates to reflect financial innovation
process_flow.description: Central banks periodically revise definitions of money supply to account for new financial instruments and changes in payment systems.
process_flow.mechanism: The Fed redefined M1 in May 2020 (moving savings deposits into M1) and discontinued M3 in 2006. Similar redefinitions occur in other countries as financial systems evolve. Analysts must be aware of structural breaks in time series.

image.alt: Timeline of redefinitions: 1980 (NOW accounts), 2006 (M3 discontinued), 2020 (savings moved to M1); future (stablecoins, CBDC).
image.caption: Figure 7.1.15: Step 5 - Periodic redefinition: money supply measures evolve with financial innovation; analysts must adjust for structural breaks.

image.alt: Master framework: Data collection → Component classification → Aggregation (M1, M2) → Public release → Market analysis → Policy assessment → Feedback to data collection; redefinition arrows showing periodic updates.
image.caption: Figure 7.1.16: Complete framework for money supply measurement: from raw data to policy use, with periodic redefinitions to maintain relevance.

summary: The money supply is measured using hierarchical aggregates based on liquidity. M1 (narrow money) includes currency in circulation, demand deposits, traveler's checks, and other checkable deposits—assets directly usable as a medium of exchange. M2 (broad money) adds near-monies: savings deposits, small time deposits (CDs under \$100,000), and retail money market mutual funds. These aggregates help central banks gauge liquidity conditions, though their predictive power for inflation has weakened since the 1990s due to financial innovation and unstable velocity. The quantity equation ($MV = PY$) relates money supply to nominal GDP via velocity. Declining velocity (from 2.0 in 1990 to 1.2 in 2023) has decoupled money growth from inflation in the short run, though extreme M2 growth (2020-2021) preceded the 2022 inflation surge. International comparisons are complicated by differing definitions: the ECB's M3 includes repos; China's M2 includes all deposits; the Fed discontinued M3 in 2006. Financial innovation (NOW accounts, MMMFs, mobile money, stablecoins) continually blurs the boundaries between narrow and broad money, requiring periodic redefinitions (e.g., the Fed's 2020 redefinition moving savings into M1). Understanding money supply measures is essential for interpreting central bank reports, analyzing liquidity conditions, and evaluating long-run inflation risks, but analysts must recognize the limitations of simple money-inflation correlations.
